This requires file-level audit, not just zone-level observation.

The sourcevision:analyzers-3 → sourcevision:unit (570 calls) plus sourcevision:unit → sourcevision:analyzers (187 calls) form a tripartite cycle: analyzers-3 depends on unit, unit depends on analyzers. If all three zones are production code, a shared analyzer interface or abstract base type is missing — the cycle will resist refactoring as the analyzer count grows.

The web-server zone (5 files, cohesion 0.75) is structurally similar to web-package-build-config post-correction: both will be small, low-import-edge clusters that Louvain may not maintain stably. Both should receive explicit zone pins to prevent drift.

The three cross-zone import edges (web-viewer → web-server) are the only runtime inter-zone coupling in the web package, yet no automated fitness function currently verifies that these three imports pass through domain-gateway.ts rather than directly hitting leaf files — the gateway rule has a verification gap for intra-package cross-zone imports, not just inter-package imports.

CONFIRMED LAYERING VIOLATION: packages/rex/src/analyze/guided.ts (domain zone packages-rex:unit-analyze) imports `info` from `../cli/output.js` (adapter zone packages-rex:cli). This is a production-code dependency from domain logic into the CLI adapter layer — a violation of the four-tier hierarchy. The fix is trivial: cli/output.ts only re-exports `info` from @n-dx/llm-client, so guided.ts should import directly from @n-dx/llm-client instead, eliminating the cross-tier import entirely.

The bidirectional call graph signal between packages-rex:unit-analyze and packages-rex:cli (239 + 100 calls) is now explained: the 239 calls are CLI invoking analyze (correct direction), the 100 inbound calls are attributable to guided.ts importing info from cli/output.ts (wrong direction). This single import line is the sole source of the layering violation.

The gateway fitness functions (check-gateway-regex.mjs, check-gateway-test.mjs) validate cross-package imports but have no coverage of intra-package cross-tier imports (domain importing from cli/). The rex guided.ts violation demonstrates a class of layering bugs the existing scripts cannot detect — a tier-aware lint rule (e.g. ESLint no-restricted-imports) would close this gap.

The e2e-tests zone and scripts zone together form a two-layer fitness function strategy, but the two layers have an asymmetric update cadence risk: scripts (4 files) are pure structural validators that rarely need updating, while e2e-tests (19 files) must track every new CLI command. As new commands are added (e.g. ndx sync, ndx usage), the e2e suite must be explicitly extended — there is no automated mechanism to detect when a new CLI surface area lacks e2e coverage.

Across all zones, the only test zone with zero coupling to production zones is e2e-tests. All other test zones (packages-rex:unit-analyze, packages-sourcevision:unit, packages-llm-client:unit) show substantial inbound call volume from their production subjects. This means e2e-tests is the only test zone that cannot accidentally be broken by a library refactor — a structural property worth explicitly preserving in future test zone placement decisions.

The four-tier dependency hierarchy (orchestration → execution → domain → foundation) is validated structurally by scripts and behaviorally by e2e-tests, but neither layer validates the HTTP transport layer introduced by ndx start. A dedicated web-server e2e test zone (currently absent) would complete the fitness function coverage across all tiers.

packages-rex:unit-analyze → packages-rex:cli (239 calls) and packages-rex:cli → packages-rex:unit-analyze (100 calls) form a bidirectional dependency between a domain zone and an adapter zone. The cli→unit-analyze direction is expected; the unit-analyze→cli direction is a potential layering violation that must be audited at the file level to confirm it is test-only.

sourcevision:analyzers-3 → sourcevision:unit (570 calls) and sourcevision:unit → sourcevision:analyzers (187 calls) create a tripartite production-zone dependency cycle with no shared abstraction layer to mediate the bidirectional relationship. As the analyzer count grows, this cycle will make it increasingly difficult to isolate or replace individual analyzers.

The gateway rule (all cross-package runtime imports through a single gateway per source package) is enforced only for inter-package imports by scripts/check-gateway-*.mjs. The 3 web-viewer → web-server intra-package cross-zone imports are not covered by any fitness function, leaving a verification gap that will grow as the web package adds more cross-zone wiring.

Existing architectural fitness functions (check-gateway-*.mjs) validate cross-package gateway conformance but have no mechanism to enforce intra-package tier ordering (e.g. domain must not import from cli/). The rex guided.ts violation went undetected because no tooling checks for domain→adapter imports within a single package. A no-restricted-imports ESLint rule per package would provide this coverage.

The recommended MCP transport is HTTP (ndx start + HTTP endpoints), but the e2e-tests zone contains no tests for HTTP MCP session lifecycle, tool dispatch over HTTP, or server start/stop behavior. The entire HTTP transport path is exercised only by manual use, creating a regression surface with no automated coverage.

Zone IDs do not follow a consistent relationship to package names: 'autonomous-agent' maps to the hench package, 'monorepo-orchestration' maps to the root, 'web-package-build-config' maps to web build files. This lack of a declared mapping convention means contributors cannot infer zone IDs from package names or vice versa — a zone-to-package index in CONTEXT.md or a naming convention doc would reduce orientation cost for new contributors.

Logo assets (Hench-F.png, Hench.png, SourceVision-F.png, SourceVision.png) are split across two code zones (autonomous-agent, web-package-build-config) despite a dedicated logo zone existing. This is a consistent cross-zone misattribution pattern — a single zone-pin policy targeting *.png files across all packages would consolidate all logo assets into the logo zone and remove static assets from code zone file counts.

The codebase has three distinct zone types — code zones (import-edge topology drives cohesion/coupling), content zones (filesystem colocation drives metrics, no import edges), and asset zones (no import edges, no filesystem colocation signal) — but sourcevision reports all three using identical metrics. Differentiating zone type in zone metadata would prevent content-zone and asset-zone cohesion=1/coupling=0 scores from inflating aggregate codebase health statistics.

The only zone with a combined low-cohesion + high-coupling fragility signal (web-package-build-config) is caused entirely by a classification error, not genuine architectural coupling. This means the codebase currently has zero zones with genuine structural fragility — a strong health signal, but one that depends on zone pins being applied before the next analysis run to remain accurate.

public.ts documents mcp-deps.ts as the gateway contract (lines 36-44) while mcp-deps.ts carries @deprecated directing consumers away from it — the package's public API documentation and its implementation are in direct conflict. This split state means the documented contract and the enforced contract diverge, which is harder to detect than a missing deprecation altogether.

mcp-deps.ts combining both rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts exports into a single import surface means any consumer that imported from mcp-deps.ts cannot be statically analyzed to determine whether it depends on rex, sourcevision, or both — the per-upstream-package gateway discipline is unenforceable through the combined barrel. This is the structural reason the @deprecated tag alone is insufficient: the violation is a design property, not just a migration lag.

The aggregation-cache.ts / usage-analytics-service split reveals a pattern: web-dashboard has absorbed infrastructure that serves a satellite zone's domain (analytics caching). As the hub-and-spoke topology matures, periodically auditing whether web-dashboard files are truly dashboard concerns vs. satellite-domain infrastructure would prevent the hub from becoming a catch-all for analytics and other satellite concerns.

The scripts zone has one script scoped to a specific package (hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs) while the other three are generic monorepo validators. If similar call-graph analysis is needed for rex or sourcevision in the future, the current naming convention implies creating rex-callgraph-analysis.mjs and sourcevision-callgraph-analysis.mjs — consider whether a single parameterized analyze-callgraph.mjs script would better serve the zone's long-term maintainability.

The scripts zone (4 files) and e2e-tests zone (19 files) have a 1:5 structural-to-behavioral fitness function ratio. Structural checks are stable and cheap to maintain; behavioral checks must track every new CLI command. As the CLI surface grows, this asymmetry will widen — new commands should prefer a structural check in scripts over a new e2e test file wherever the constraint is expressible at the import-graph level.

No shared test-utilities zone exists across the monorepo. Each test zone (e2e-tests, packages-rex:unit, packages-sourcevision:unit, packages-llm-client:unit) is self-contained, which is correct for isolation, but process-spawning boilerplate and fixture helpers are likely duplicated across at least the e2e and integration test files. A tests/shared/ utilities module that contains no production imports would reduce duplication without introducing coupling to any production zone.

The four-tier hierarchy is validated top-to-bottom by two mechanisms — scripts (structural) and e2e-tests (behavioral) — but validation is asymmetric: the orchestration and execution tiers have behavioral e2e coverage, the domain tier (rex, sourcevision) has unit coverage, and the foundation tier (llm-client) has unit coverage, but the HTTP transport tier introduced by ndx start has no automated validation at any layer. This creates a tier-shaped coverage gap precisely at the boundary that is now the recommended integration path.

Reconcile the mcp-deps.ts documentation split: public.ts presents it as canonical gateway while mcp-deps.ts marks it @deprecated. Pick one source of truth — either remove the @deprecated and accept mcp-deps.ts as the stable combined gateway, or update public.ts to document rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts as the canonical gateways and add an eslint no-restricted-imports rule to block new mcp-deps.ts imports.

hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs is the only monorepo script scoped to a single package. If call-graph analysis becomes a recurring need across packages, parameterize it into a generic analyze-callgraph.mjs --package=hench script to avoid proliferating per-package analysis scripts with duplicated logic.

Add a meta-test in the scripts zone (or e2e-tests) that verifies architecture-policy.test.js and scripts/check-*.mjs do not enforce contradictory or redundant rules. Two fitness function layers checking the same constraint via different mechanisms will eventually diverge; a reconciliation test or shared constraint registry would keep them synchronized.

Introduce a tests/shared/ directory for process-spawning utilities and CLI fixture helpers shared across e2e test files. This directory should import only Node built-ins and test framework APIs — no production package imports — to preserve the zero-coupling property of the test zones.

The e2e-tests zone covers ndx ci, config, delegation, dev, errors, and CLI contracts but has no visible coverage for ndx sync, ndx usage, or ndx start (HTTP server lifecycle). As these commands graduate from new to stable, the absence of e2e coverage for them represents an expanding regression surface with no automated backstop.

Dead-export pattern appears across 5 separate files in 3 different packages (findings 8-12), suggesting a project-wide habit of leaving superseded exports in place rather than removing them at the call-site. This is low individual risk but cumulative maintenance debt.

The only anti-pattern finding with concrete metrics (finding 13: cmdRecommend at 36 unique calls) is isolated to a single CLI command and has no corroborating structural findings from other passes, which suggests it is a bounded problem, not a systemic CLI architecture issue.

All high-cohesion zones (cohesion=1) are either very small (3–8 files) or single-purpose (scripts, logo, e2e). The one zone with measurable coupling (web at 0.41) is also the integration layer by design. The metrics collectively indicate healthy zone decomposition, not a decomposition problem.

The schema/index.ts fan-in (finding 1) and walkTree fan-in (finding 7) are both flagged as risks but represent opposite design intents: schema/index is a barrel intended to be imported widely, and walkTree is a core traversal primitive expected to be universally referenced. Neither warrants remediation.

Finding 1 flags packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts as a high-impact hotspot due to 22 callers, but barrel/index exports are architecturally intended to accumulate callers — that is their sole purpose. The finding contradicts the project's documented Public API convention ('src/public.ts → exports["."] in package.json'). Finding 1 is incorrect as a risk signal; the fan-in is a success indicator, not a warning.

Finding 4 (mcp-deps.ts @deprecated vs public.ts canonical gateway claim) and finding 2 (architecture-policy.test.js vs check-*.mjs enforcement divergence) both represent the same class of problem: two authoritative sources for the same constraint drifting out of sync. The actionable fix for finding 4 is: open packages/web/src/server/mcp-deps.ts, confirm whether any file outside the gateway pattern still imports from it, then either remove the @deprecated annotation and update public.ts commentary, or add an eslint no-restricted-imports rule pointing to packages/web/src/server/rex-gateway.ts and packages/web/src/server/domain-gateway.ts as the only permitted cross-package import paths. The actionable fix for finding 2 is: add a single test in tests/e2e/ that imports both scripts/check-circular.mjs and packages/sourcevision/src/tests/unit/architecture-policy.test.ts constraints and asserts they do not overlap on the same import pair.

Five dead-export candidates span packages/web/src/viewer/polling/polling-restart.ts (3 exports), packages/web/src/viewer/loader.ts (2 exports), packages/hench/src/shared/glob.ts, packages/sourcevision/src/analyzers/branch-work-filter.ts, and packages/sourcevision/src/cli/commands/prd-epic-resolver.ts. Before removing any, verify they are not referenced via dynamic import, re-exported through a barrel not captured by the call-graph scan, or used exclusively in test files (which static dead-export scans may not traverse). Audit in this order: search for string-based dynamic imports first, then remove confirmed dead exports package by package.

The 142 cross-file call count between packages/web/src/server/routes-rex.ts and packages/web/src/server/types.ts (finding 14) is unusually high even for a route-to-types dependency. Combined with the god-function finding for cmdRecommend (finding 13, 36 unique calls), both signals point to the rex CLI and web server layers accumulating complexity without extraction. These are independent files in different packages but share a root cause: command handlers and route handlers are each absorbing orchestration logic that could be pushed into dedicated service or use-case modules. This is a warning-level pattern when the two findings are read together.

Findings 1 and 4 directly contradict each other on packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts: finding 1 flags its 22-caller fan-in as a warning-level hotspot while finding 4 correctly establishes that barrel/index exports accumulating callers is the documented and intended Public API convention. Barrel files should be excluded from automated fan-in detection heuristics to prevent systematic false positives.

Dead-export candidates appear independently across three separate packages (packages/web, packages/hench, packages/sourcevision — findings 7–11), indicating no automated unused-export check runs in CI. This is a project-wide gap rather than isolated noise in individual packages.

Two distinct 'dual-enforcement drift' problems surface independently in finding 5: the mcp-deps.ts @deprecated annotation diverging from the gateway canon, and check-circular.mjs diverging from architecture-policy.test.ts. Both are instances of the same meta-pattern where a constraint is asserted in two places that evolve independently. The systemic fix is one authoritative source per constraint — either a living test or a linter rule, not both in parallel.

Finding 12 (cmdRecommend, 36 unique outgoing calls) is the only finding in this pass corroborated by two independent signals: the automated call-graph heuristic and the LLM cross-analysis in finding 3. Every other warning-level heuristic finding stands on a single signal only, which per the evaluation constraints is insufficient for escalation.

Finding 12 (cmdRecommend, 36 unique outgoing calls) is corroborated by both an automated call-graph heuristic and independent LLM cross-analysis in finding 3 — the only finding with dual-signal confirmation. This makes warning severity well-founded. At 36 calls the metric is approximately 1.8x a typical detection threshold and does not yet meet the 2x bar for concrete decomposition prescriptions; however, if the outgoing-call count grows past ~40 as the recommend command gains features, extraction of orchestration logic into a dedicated service layer within packages/rex/src/cli/commands/ becomes concretely justified.

Finding 1 (warning: packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts, 22-caller fan-in hotspot) is a false positive directly contradicted by finding 4 (info). The 22-caller count is a success indicator for the project's documented Public API convention — barrel exports are architecturally intended to accumulate callers. Finding 1's severity should be info, not warning. Automated fan-in heuristics should exclude files named index.ts or public.ts to avoid this class of false positive.

Finding 1 (fan-in hotspot on packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts) is a confirmed false positive: findings 5 and 6 independently identify it as a success indicator for the documented Public API convention, not a risk. Downgraded to info. Automated fan-in heuristics should exclude files named index.ts or public.ts to prevent recurrence.

Finding 15 (tightly coupled modules: packages/web/src/server/routes-rex.ts and packages/web/src/server/types.ts, 142 cross-file calls) is upgraded to warning because finding 4 provides LLM corroboration identifying it as part of a dual-signal pattern alongside the cmdRecommend god-function finding — both reflecting the same root cause of orchestration logic accumulation in handler layers.

Two independent dual-authority-drift instances exist in the project: (a) packages/web/src/server/mcp-deps.ts @deprecated annotation vs canonical gateway claim in public.ts, and (b) packages/sourcevision/src/tests/unit/architecture-policy.test.ts constraints vs scripts/check-circular.mjs enforcement. These represent the same class of problem — constraint documentation outpacing enforcement tooling — and should be resolved together rather than independently.

Dead-export candidates in findings 9–13 span 5 files across 3 packages (packages/web, packages/hench, packages/sourcevision). The distribution suggests a systemic gap in call-graph traversal coverage (dynamic imports and barrel re-exports may not be traversed), not necessarily dead code. All 5 require manual verification before removal.

All cohesion-1 zone-level findings (root, .rex, docs, hench, web-landing, logo, scripts, e2e) are positive architecture indicators. Tight intra-zone interconnection is the intended outcome of community detection and requires no action.

cmdRecommend (finding 14, 36 unique outgoing calls, approximately 1.8x detection threshold) and routes-rex.ts/types.ts coupling (finding 15, 142 cross-file calls) share a root cause confirmed by LLM cross-analysis (finding 4): both packages/rex/src/cli/commands/recommend.ts and packages/web/src/server/routes-rex.ts are absorbing orchestration logic that belongs in dedicated service or use-case modules. Neither metric currently exceeds 2x its detection threshold, so concrete file decomposition is not prescribed. New features added to either file should be directed into named service modules within their respective package src/ directories (e.g. packages/rex/src/cli/commands/ for rex, packages/web/src/server/ for web) to prevent threshold breach and avoid a future critical finding.

Dead-export audit sequence for findings 9–13: (1) grep all packages for string-based dynamic imports referencing polling-restart.ts, loader.ts, glob.ts, branch-work-filter.ts, and prd-epic-resolver.ts by filename; (2) search barrel and re-export files in each package for the specific symbol names (isPollingRestartStarted, isCoordinatorSuspended, resetPollingRestart, loadModule, loadFromServer, simpleGlobMatch, detectBranchLifecycle, resolveWorkedEpicTitlesForRange); (3) check test files for direct usage of each symbol. Audit in ascending risk order: packages/hench/src/shared/glob.ts (single symbol, isolated package) → packages/sourcevision/src/analyzers/branch-work-filter.ts and prd-epic-resolver.ts → packages/web/src/viewer/loader.ts → packages/web/src/viewer/polling/polling-restart.ts. Only remove after all three checks confirm no usage.

Resolve mcp-deps.ts @deprecated drift (finding 7, first case): open packages/web/src/server/mcp-deps.ts, enumerate its exports, then grep the web package src/ tree for any import of those symbols outside packages/web/src/server/rex-gateway.ts and packages/web/src/server/domain-gateway.ts. If no external callers remain, delete mcp-deps.ts entirely. If callers remain, add an eslint no-restricted-imports rule pointing to rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts as the only permitted cross-package import paths and emit an error on direct mcp-deps.ts imports. Do not leave the @deprecated annotation as the sole enforcement mechanism.

Resolve architecture-policy constraint drift (finding 7, second case): add a single test in the sourcevision e2e suite that imports the constraint sets from both packages/sourcevision/src/tests/unit/architecture-policy.test.ts and scripts/check-circular.mjs and asserts they do not define overlapping rules for the same import pair with different outcomes. This prevents the two enforcement paths from silently diverging as new cross-package imports are added.

Two distinct enforcement mechanisms (scripts/check-circular.mjs and packages/sourcevision/src/tests/unit/architecture-policy.test.ts) govern the same constraint space without a shared source of truth. This is a systemic drift risk: as new cross-package imports are added, one path may silently miss a rule the other enforces. The fix is a single convergence test, not maintenance of two parallel rule sets.

Dead exports are accumulating independently in four separate packages (web, hench, sourcevision) with no unified audit process. Individually each is low-risk, but collectively they indicate the call-graph boundary is not being enforced at merge time. A single pre-commit or CI step that runs the dead-export scan on touched packages would prevent accumulation.

The fan-in hotspot heuristic incorrectly fires on barrel/index files by design. packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts and similar public.ts files are architecturally intended to accumulate callers — this is the documented Public API convention. The heuristic should be suppressed for files named index.ts or public.ts to eliminate this class of false positive before it pollutes future reports.

cmdRecommend (36 unique outgoing calls, ~1.8x threshold) and routes-rex.ts (142 cross-file calls to types.ts) are independently identified by both automated heuristics and LLM cross-analysis as absorbing orchestration logic. Neither exceeds the 2x threshold for mandatory decomposition, but both are on the same growth trajectory and share the same root cause: command/route handlers acting as use-case orchestrators rather than thin dispatchers. This pattern should be monitored — the next feature added to either file should land in a named service module, not the handler itself.

Global finding 1 (packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts, 22-caller fan-in, warning severity) is a confirmed false positive explicitly contradicted by global finding 7. Barrel exports named index.ts or public.ts are the project's documented Public API convention; accumulating callers is the intended outcome, not a risk. Severity is correctly info. The automated heuristic should exclude files matching these name patterns to prevent recurrence in subsequent analysis passes.

The three dead-export findings in packages/web/src/viewer/ (polling-restart.ts: isPollingRestartStarted, isCoordinatorSuspended, resetPollingRestart; loader.ts: loadModule, loadFromServer) are co-located in the viewer package and share a common audit path. Before removal: (1) grep the web src/ tree for string-based dynamic imports referencing either filename; (2) check packages/web/src/viewer/index.ts and any barrel file for re-exports of these symbols; (3) search web test files for direct symbol usage. If all three checks return empty, both files can be cleaned in a single PR. This is lower risk than the hench and sourcevision dead exports because they are confined to one package.

Resolve mcp-deps.ts @deprecated drift (global finding 10) with the following concrete steps: open packages/web/src/server/mcp-deps.ts and list its exported symbols; run grep -r 'mcp-deps' packages/web/src/ excluding rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts; if the result is empty, delete mcp-deps.ts entirely and remove any re-export references; if callers remain, add an ESLint no-restricted-imports rule in packages/web/.eslintrc.* that errors on direct imports from ./mcp-deps and points to rex-gateway.ts or domain-gateway.ts as the replacement. The @deprecated annotation alone provides no enforcement and will silently accumulate violations.

Resolve architecture-policy constraint drift (global finding 9) by adding one test file at packages/sourcevision/tests/e2e/architecture-constraints-sync.test.ts that imports the rule sets from both scripts/check-circular.mjs and packages/sourcevision/src/tests/unit/architecture-policy.test.ts and asserts: (a) no import pair is governed by rules in both files with different outcomes, and (b) any pair governed by only one file is explicitly documented as intentional. This test fails fast when either enforcement path gains a rule the other does not mirror, making drift immediately visible in CI.

Global finding 1 (schema/index.ts fan-in, warning) is a confirmed false positive explicitly contradicted by global findings 4 and 8. Barrel exports named index.ts or public.ts are the project's documented Public API convention; 22 callers is a success indicator, not a risk. Severity must be info. The automated heuristic should exclude files matching these name patterns to prevent recurrence.

Global findings 9, 10, and 11 describe the same architecture-policy constraint drift issue. Finding 11 is the most actionable — it names a concrete output file (packages/sourcevision/tests/e2e/architecture-constraints-sync.test.ts) and two specific assertions. Findings 9 and 10 are redundant subsets of finding 11 and should be treated as resolved by it.

Global findings 9 (partial), 12, and 13 all describe the same mcp-deps.ts @deprecated enforcement gap. With four independent mentions across analysis passes, this is the highest-frequency unresolved item. Finding 13 is the most actionable version. The @deprecated annotation is not enforcement; the only outcomes that close this finding are: (a) delete mcp-deps.ts after confirming zero callers outside the two gateway files, or (b) add an ESLint no-restricted-imports rule pointing to rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts as the only permitted cross-package import paths.

Dead-export candidates receive independent confirmation from both automated heuristics (findings 16–20) and LLM analysis (findings 3, 7, 14). The audit sequence in finding 7 (hench glob.ts → sourcevision branch-work-filter.ts + prd-epic-resolver.ts → web loader.ts → web polling-restart.ts) is the correct execution order by blast-radius risk and should be followed as written.

Both outstanding warning-level systemic issues (mcp-deps.ts @deprecated drift and architecture-policy constraint drift) share a structural root cause: the project has correct architecture documented in CLAUDE.md but lacks mechanical cross-source consistency checks. Neither is a complexity or coupling problem. Both are resolved by adding enforcement rather than refactoring: one ESLint rule or file deletion, and one new sync test file.

Contradiction: global finding 1 (warning, schema/index.ts 22-caller fan-in hotspot) directly contradicts global findings 4 and 8 (both info, confirmed false positive). Finding 1's severity must be info. The automated fan-in heuristic should be updated to exclude files named index.ts and public.ts project-wide to prevent this class of false positive from recurring in subsequent analysis passes.

Redundancy cluster: global findings 9, 10, and 11 are three restatements of the architecture-policy constraint drift issue. Finding 11 supersedes both with a named output path (packages/sourcevision/tests/e2e/architecture-constraints-sync.test.ts) and two concrete assertions. Resolving finding 11 closes all three. Findings 9 and 10 should be marked resolved when finding 11's test is added.

Redundancy cluster with highest recurrence: mcp-deps.ts @deprecated drift appears in global findings 9 (partial), 12, and 13 plus an indirect reference in finding 4 — four independent mentions, the most of any single issue. Concrete resolution steps from finding 13: run grep -r 'mcp-deps' packages/web/src/ excluding packages/web/src/server/rex-gateway.ts and packages/web/src/server/domain-gateway.ts; if the result is empty, delete packages/web/src/server/mcp-deps.ts entirely and remove any barrel re-exports referencing it; if callers remain, add a no-restricted-imports ESLint rule in packages/web/.eslintrc.* that errors on direct mcp-deps imports and names rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts as replacements. Completing this step closes findings 9, 12, and 13 simultaneously.

The two warning-level enforcement gaps (mcp-deps.ts drift and architecture-policy drift) both follow the same pattern: a correct architectural decision is documented in prose (CLAUDE.md gateway rules; circular-dependency policy) but has no mechanical check that keeps two sources of truth in sync. The fix in both cases is additive enforcement, not structural refactoring. Neither finding requires touching domain logic or decomposing existing modules.

Global finding 1 (schema/index.ts fan-in warning) is a confirmed false positive per finding 2: barrel exports named index.ts and public.ts are designed for high fan-in. Downgraded to info; the automated heuristic should exclude files whose sole purpose is re-exporting.

mcp-deps.ts has zero runtime import callers: grep of packages/web/src for 'from.*mcp-deps' returns no matches. The two hits in public.ts and types.ts are JSDoc @see comments only. The file can be deleted and those comments updated — no import-site migration is required.

The 'documentation-without-enforcement' anti-pattern is the systemic root cause shared by findings 3, 4, and 5: gateway rules are in CLAUDE.md prose only; circular-dependency policy lacks an architecture test. Both gaps are closed by additive tooling (ESLint rule + one test file), not structural refactoring.

All zones except 'web' (cohesion 0.59) and 'web-server' (cohesion 0.75) show cohesion ≥ 0.98. The two lower-cohesion zones are the smallest boundary-layer zones by file count and represent intentional integration surfaces, not decomposition problems.

simpleGlobMatch duplicate in hench: packages/hench/src/shared/glob.ts exports simpleGlobMatch (global finding 11) but packages/hench/src/tools/files.ts imports simpleGlobMatch directly from ../guard/paths.js, and guard/index.ts re-exports it from the same source. The shared/glob.ts copy has no callers — it is a stale duplicate superseded during a guard-module refactor. Delete packages/hench/src/shared/glob.ts entirely; no call sites need updating.

Dead-export cluster (global findings 9–13) spans 3 packages with no cross-package pattern linking them — each is an isolated orphan. The hench case (simpleGlobMatch in shared/glob.ts) is a confirmed duplicate with a clear deletion path. The two sourcevision cases (detectBranchLifecycle, resolveWorkedEpicTitlesForRange) and two web cases (loader.ts, polling-restart.ts) are single-heuristic signals that require manual verification of dynamic or external callers before removal. Zone "Analyzers 3" (packages-sourcevision:analyzers-3) has critical risk (score: 0.69, cohesion: 0.31, coupling: 0.69) — justified: Core zone analysis engine (Louvain algorithm, zone detection, zone hashing) with 3 test files.
